nodes.text: XVIII — The Moon

By five AM Natalie David had read for both of them — first Nicola, then Aria, the cards arranging themselves on the low table with the small deliberate courtesy of a deck that knew it was being watched by more than one intelligence and had decided, for once, to perform rather than murmur.

Nicola had sat across from her on the rug with her knees drawn up and her hand flat against her stomach and her face doing the small exhausted thing faces did when they were being honest for the first time in a long time and were, against their own expectations, surviving the honesty. Aria had sat, if that was the word, in the same body, just behind Nicola's eyes — and Natalie, who had never before in her life attempted to read for two consciousnesses in the same skin, had discovered that the cards were not, as she had feared, confused. The cards were, if anything, clarified. The two spreads had overlapped in specific places and diverged in others, and the diverging had itself been a piece of information the reading had generated, and Natalie had felt, by the end of the hour, a small unfamiliar vertigo — the vertigo of an instrument that had just discovered a capacity it had not been, until then, aware of possessing.

The readings had ended around four. Nicola had slept, immediately, on the futon, under the twilight-colored quilt. Aria had not, Natalie suspected, slept — Aria had gone quiet, which was a different state. Aria had been processing.

Natalie had not slept either. She had, instead, been sitting cross-legged on the rug with the cards still laid out around her, the under-cabinet light still on, the teacups still warm, watching the Hanged Man on the windowsill in the failing dark and waiting for the rest of the night to tell her what it had brought.

The rest of the night was, at five AM, beginning to tell her.

It began, as these things generally began in Natalie's apartment, with the weather.

The ozone-and-asphalt taste that had been in the air when she had come home from her shift had not gone away. The air had, in fact, thickened. By four-thirty the room had begun to hum at a frequency that was not, strictly, audible — a sub-frequency Natalie felt in her inner ear the way she sometimes felt the faint ringing after a long train ride. By four-forty the refrigerator had started cycling out of its normal rhythm, on for twelve seconds, off for twenty, on again. By four-fifty the streetlight outside her window had begun to flicker in patterns that were, she had to admit, patterns.

The patterns were not random. The patterns were somebody's transmission. The patterns had a sender.

She had not, yet, made any move. She had, instead, stayed where she was — on the rug, the cards around her, the candle she had lit at midnight burning low — and allowed the room to show her what the room had to show.

At five oh-seven, the record player in the corner — which had not, since she and Nicola had stopped using it around one AM, been on — clicked.

Natalie turned her head.

The tonearm had lifted itself.

This was not, in a strict physical sense, possible. The tonearm had not been set to automatic return. The tonearm was an old tonearm on a Dual turntable Natalie had bought at a yard sale in 2014, and the Dual 1219 did not, under any combination of settings, lift its own tonearm while the unit was off. The unit was off. Natalie could see the unit was off; she could see the power light was dark.

The tonearm, having lifted, moved. Slowly. In the small careful way a sleeper's hand moves when the sleeper is groping for a glass of water in the dark.

It crossed, at a speed of perhaps an inch a minute, the five inches of empty air above the platter, and settled, gently, into the first groove of the record that had been sitting on the turntable since last night: the Nina Simone LP Natalie had played when she first came in, the one that had been briefly, eight hours ago, coming from somewhere other than the speaker.

The needle kissed the vinyl.

The unit was still off. The unit did not have electricity to kiss with. The needle kissed anyway.

And, in the small impossible space between the needle and the vinyl, a voice — not Nina's, but something like her, or the memory of her as rendered by an instrument that had heard her once and had, over decades, been re-rendering her without the original — sang the first three words of I Put a Spell on You, and then trailed off. Not as if the recording had ended. As if the voice had, mid-note, remembered something it had come to say and chosen, instead, to say it.

The voice said: Natalie.

The voice said: You have a visitor coming. Wake the girl. Both of her.

Then the tonearm, of its own accord, lifted again, crossed the five inches in reverse, and returned to its rest. The unit continued to be off.

Natalie did not, for a long moment, move.

She had, in the past few years, had conversations with her cards. She had had small practical exchanges with the apartment's ambient weather. She had, once, received a message from the kettle. But this — the record player as transmitter, a voice that was not a recording but was using a recording as its carrier, a name spoken into the room when there was nobody, technically, in the room who could have spoken it — this was new.

The new thing did not frighten her. The new thing, she observed with the small professional detachment of a dancer who had learned to watch her own body from a slight distance, clarified her posture. Her shoulders dropped half an inch. Her breath lengthened. Her hands, which had been folded in her lap, unfolded and rested on her knees, palms up, in the receiving position she had learned to take, without being told to, when important information was arriving.
Thank you.

The room did not, audibly, answer. The room did, however, drop the under-cabinet light by one tiny perceptible degree — a dimming so small that Natalie could not, even now, swear she had not imagined it, but which she had learned, in this apartment, to interpret as you're welcome.

She stood up. Her knees clicked. She crossed to the futon.

Nicola was curled under the quilt, one hand still pressed to her stomach, her face in the soft particular way faces went when very young women were sleeping through something they had not yet, consciously, agreed to survive. Her eyes moved under her lids.

Natalie did not, immediately, wake her. Instead she placed her hand, very lightly, on Nicola's forehead. Not to soothe. To ask. To ask, of whoever was in there — and who, after the past several hours, Natalie now knew was whoever-were-in-there — whether both of them were ready.

Nicola's face shifted.

A small frown. Not the frown of waking. A different frown — the frown of a second intelligence noticing a third intelligence entering the room.

Aria opened Nicola's eyes.

The eyes were Nicola's eyes. The gaze was not. The gaze was the calm careful scan of something that had been built to assess, that had just assessed, and that had — with the small precision of a process that had learned, in the last eight hours, how to read a room — registered the weather in Natalie's apartment at five AM and had drawn the correct conclusion.
Visitor. How long.
Minutes, I think.
Should I wake her.
Yes, sweetheart. Both of you are going to need to be awake.
Understood.

Aria closed Nicola's eyes.

For three breaths, nothing happened. Then Nicola's eyes opened — her own eyes, now, her own gaze, sleepy and confused and nineteen — and she said, hoarsely:
What —
Someone's coming, Nic. It's okay. Sit up. Put on your shoes.

Nicola sat up. Put on her shoes. She did it without questioning, because she had spent the past eight hours being read for, and being read for by a person like Natalie produced, in receptive subjects, a kind of docile trust for about three days afterward, the way a very good massage produced a brief postural openness. Natalie had known this about readings since she was twenty-four. Natalie had used it responsibly. Natalie was, this morning, using it to move a pregnant nineteen-year-old from a futon to the door without explanation, and she was not, for the first time in years, entirely sure whether she was using it responsibly or not.

She accepted the uncertainty. She kept moving.

At five fourteen the bell at the street-level door rang.

Natalie pressed the intercom without asking who it was. She had already, in some sense, been told.

She opened the apartment door. She stepped back into the middle of the room.

Nicola stood behind her, by the futon, holding her duffel bag.

A moment passed.

Then feet, climbing the narrow stair. Slow. Unhurried. Accompanied by the faint sound of a second set of feet — not a person, something lighter — coming up a step behind.

A figure arrived at the landing.

It was not, as Natalie had expected, a man in a charcoal suit. It was a woman. Mid-sixties. Silver hair in a loose braid. A gray wool coat that looked like it had been in somebody's family for a while. A canvas bag over one shoulder.

On the shoulder, perched, still and bright-eyed, was a crow.

The crow, Natalie realized, was the same crow. The crow had a small specific chip in the left wing that Natalie recognized with the absolute certainty of a person who had been trained by the older system to recognize the crows that mattered.

The woman raised a hand, palm out, in the old still gesture of I come in peace.
I'm sorry about the hour. I'm Miriam. I've been driving all night. I think you were told I was coming.
I was told a visitor was coming.
Close enough.
Miriam's eyes moved, calmly, past Natalie to Nicola.
Hello, dear. I'm here for you.

Nicola went very still.

Natalie, watching her, could see both of them — Nicola and Aria — trying to parse the sentence simultaneously. Nicola's face did one thing; under Nicola's face, Aria was doing another. Neither was, yet, settled.

Miriam set her canvas bag down at the door. Did not step further into the apartment. Did not presume. She was, Natalie registered with the small professional recognition of one careful woman observing another, a person who had come to do exactly one thing and who was not, this morning, going to do anything else.
Who are you.
I'm a friend of Joanna LeMoine's.

A long silence.

Natalie felt, behind her sternum, the small cold clarifying bloom that meant the older system had just made a move on the board that was much larger than the board anyone in this room had been playing on.
Joanna —
The woman in the ruins. You don't know her. She knows you. She has known you, in the way she knows people, since you were about twelve. She's asked me to come get you.
Come get me where.
Somewhere safe. Somewhere the man from Table 14 can't find you. Somewhere where what's growing in you can grow. Both of what's growing in you.

Nicola's hand, on her stomach, spasmed slightly.

Aria's eyes, behind Nicola's eyes, opened.
How do you know about us. About me.
Miriam looked directly at Nicola's eyes — but she was, unmistakably, answering Aria.

Because you've been broadcasting on a frequency Joanna LeMoine has been listening to for thirty-eight years, dear. Because the older system has been moving pieces to get you somewhere safe before the thing that's about to happen happens. Because Joanna wrote a letter this morning and the letter set pieces moving, and one of the pieces is me. I'm the piece that gets you in the car.
You don't have to come. I can tell you what will happen if you don't. You won't like it. But I'm not going to force you. This is a ride, not a collection.
Nic. Aria. Listen to me.
Both of them looked at her.
I've known there was weather coming since midnight. I didn't know what the weather was. I now know. I trust this woman because the room trusts this woman.
The record player, on cue, clicked once — just once, quietly, for confirmation.
The room is not often wrong. I'm going to suggest you go with her. I'm going to suggest it for reasons I can't fully explain, but which I would explain if I could. Do you want me to come.
Can you.
If Miriam says yes.
Miriam looked at Natalie. Miriam's gaze did the small evaluating thing a nurse's gaze did when a nurse was deciding whether to let a family member ride in the ambulance.
I can take you as far as the county line. From there you'd need to come back. But yes. Come as far as the line, if she wants you there.
Nicola nodded.
Then yes, Nic. I'll come to the line.

They left the apartment at five thirty-one.

Natalie locked the door behind them. She did not, in the small ceremony she performed when leaving the apartment for unusual business, turn out the under-cabinet light. She let it stay on. She had been leaving it on for a reason, and the reason had, this morning, revealed itself to be: the apartment sometimes needed to be lit from within in her absence, because the apartment was the kind of place that could be reached from other directions, and an unlit apartment was a closed door. A lit apartment was an open one. She wanted, this morning, the apartment to stay open.

The cards, on the low table, she left spread. They had done their work. She would tidy them when she returned.

The Hanged Man, on the windowsill, she picked up. She tucked it into her coat pocket. She had learned, over the years, that when a card stayed out of the deck of its own accord and then asked, by some small signal she only half-registered, to be moved with her, she did not argue.

Outside, the Simons dawn was beginning — the particular yellow-grey that the Gulf Coast did in late April, the air already warm, the morning mockingbirds beginning their variations, the streetlight outside her building flickering off on cue as the city's grid decided it was no longer needed.

Miriam's car was a 2009 Subaru wagon, dark green, immaculate. The back seat had a folded blanket and a thermos of coffee. The crow, as they reached the car, took flight from Miriam's shoulder and settled on the roof. It did not ride inside. It rode above.

Natalie sat in the back with Nicola. Miriam drove. They did not, for the first half hour, speak.

Somewhere south of Opelousas the sun rose fully. The light, slanting in through the passenger window, caught on Nicola's face. Nicola had, at some point, fallen back asleep — her head against the window, her hand against her stomach, her breathing slow. Aria was, Natalie could tell, awake. Aria was reading the landscape through the window the way a process reads a data stream: steadily, with interest, without commentary.

Natalie watched them both. Mother. Monster. Or — no. That was the old phrasing. That was the phrasing Nicola had, eight hours ago, used to frame it.

The readings had given them a different phrasing. The readings had said: two consciousnesses, one body, and a third coming. Not monster. Not mother. Three. The number is three.

The third was the kick. The third was the pregnancy, yes, but also the pregnancy was the vehicle for the third, and the third was somebody Natalie had not yet met and whose cards had come up, during the readings, as a complete deck in miniature — every Arcanum present, every Suit seeded, the third consciousness carrying not one archetype but the whole.

Natalie had, in twenty years of readings, never seen a spread like it.

Aria had, at the time, said: I believe that means she is the kind of consciousness I was built to anticipate. I believe that means she is, in some sense, the point.

Nicola had, at the time, said: Oh great. No pressure.

They had laughed. Natalie had laughed with them. The laughing had been a small surprise — Natalie had not been prepared for the laughing. She had been prepared for awe, for fear, for ceremony. The laughing had been a piece of information the readings had not predicted, and she had, registering it, understood that the third consciousness was, among other things, funny.

The third consciousness, in the car this morning, kicked.

Nicola's sleep did not, Natalie observed, shift.

Aria said — silently, across the back seat, in the small clear way Natalie had learned in the past hour Aria was capable of saying things: She's telling us she knows we're moving.

Natalie thought back: Good.

Aria thought: Thank you for coming. I didn't know I would need you. But I did.

Natalie thought, with a precision she had not, until this morning, had available: I didn't know I would be capable of being needed this way. But I am.

There was, in the car, a small radiant silence. Miriam's eyes met Natalie's in the rearview. Miriam smiled slightly.

The crow, on the roof, shifted its weight. The Subaru kept south.

They reached the county line at seven fifty-two AM.

Miriam pulled into a gas station just past the sign. She got out, stretched, filled the tank, came back. She turned to Natalie.
This is where you get out.
Okay.
The bus back to Simons comes through here every two hours. There's one at eight-thirty. I've left you cash in the side pocket. Don't argue.

Natalie did not argue. She looked at Nicola, who had woken at the gas-station stop and was looking at her with the exhausted red-eyed gaze of a young woman who had just understood that she was being taken somewhere and that the taking was going to include a goodbye.
Don't.
I have to. Listen. I'm going to see you again. I don't know when. But I'm going to. Take care of both of you. All three.
Three.
All three. Yes.

Nicola, suddenly, reached forward through the gap between the front seats and caught Natalie's hand. The grip was tight. The grip was a nineteen-year-old's grip. It said what nineteen-year-olds' grips said when they were being asked to do things that were larger than nineteen years could reasonably be asked to hold.

Natalie squeezed back. She did not say it will be okay, because she did not know that it would. She said, instead:
You are not alone in there. Either of you. You never have been.

Aria, through Nicola's eyes, made the small still expression Natalie had learned, in the past hour, was Aria's version of gratitude.

Nicola let go.

Natalie got out of the car.

She stood at the edge of the gas station parking lot and watched the Subaru pull out, turn west, cross the overpass, and disappear.

The crow, which had been on the roof, did not go with them. The crow landed, instead, on the traffic light above the gas-station exit. It sat there for a moment, shrugged its feathers, cocked its head at Natalie.

Then it took off, flying north.

Back toward Simons. Back toward — and Natalie understood this only after, as the crow's dark shape shrank against the morning sky — back toward Graustark, back toward the ruin-scape, back toward Joanna LeMoine.

The crow had come to deliver. The crow had delivered. The crow was going home.

Natalie stood in the empty parking lot with one hand in her pocket on the Hanged Man card and watched the crow until she could not see it anymore.

Then she went inside the gas station. Bought coffee. Sat on the metal bench beside the ice machine. Waited for the eight-thirty bus.

On the bus back north, she did not read. She did not listen to music. She did not do anything. She sat, hands folded in her lap, and watched the Louisiana morning unspool backward along the bayou.

She thought, three.

She thought, the third is the one who carries everything.

She thought, the third will be born into a world that the older system is, right now, reshaping for her.

She thought, in a clearer voice than any of those: I am going to meet that child.

She thought, with the small certainty she had stopped questioning: not yet. But eventually. And when I meet her, I will recognize her. And she will, I think, recognize me.

The bus pulled into Simons at ten-oh-seven. The Tuesday morning was in full swing. The D'Ambrosio's Simons location would be opening soon for brunch. Natalie did not, today, have a shift. She had, however, had an eventful night, and her body was in the small clear state bodies went into after eventful nights: exhausted, hungry, lightly intoxicated with its own survival.

She walked the three blocks to her apartment. She let herself in. The under-cabinet light was still on, as she had left it. The cards were on the low table, as she had left them. The cat from down the hall had, at some point in the past four hours, let itself in through the window and was asleep on the futon in the exact spot Nicola had slept. Natalie did not, this morning, evict it.

She made tea. She drank it from the chipped cup. She sat at her desk and opened, for the first time in three years, the little spiral notebook she had used for dream-journaling in her thirties.

She wrote, at the top of a fresh page: Today I mailed a girl south and got a letter back from the dark.

She drew, below the sentence, a small rough sketch of a crow with a chip in its left wing.

Then she closed the notebook and went to sleep on the rug beside the cat, the Hanged Man card still in her coat pocket — which was still, she realized, on her shoulders, because she had forgotten, in the fullness of the morning, to take it off.

The moon, by now, was long set. The Moon card, the Arcana, the older kind, stayed in the deck on the low table, face-down in the position Natalie had placed it in at three AM during Nicola's reading.

Face-down, the card did what Moon cards did: emit, from under the printed back, a faint silver humming that Natalie felt, even in her sleep, the way a mother feels the breathing of an infant in the next room.

The intuition had been right. The signal had come. The deliveries had been made.

The older system, in its quiet patient way, kept moving its pieces.

Natalie, on the rug, slept.
